participants.extracted: Pregnant women aged 16 to 28 weeks’ gestation attending antenatal care at Shampande Health Centre or Choma Hospital in Choma District, Zambia, who agreed to HIV testing and consented to participate. Women had to be willing to deliver at the health facility and comply with monthly antenatal follow-up. Exclusions included sulfa allergy, major illnesses likely to affect pregnancy outcome (such as diabetes, severe renal or heart disease, or active tuberculosis), intention to leave the catchment area before delivery, severe anemia (hemoglobin <7 g/dL), and a previous history of unfavorable pregnancy outcome. A total of 421 women were screened, 346 met eligibility criteria, and one additional patient was recruited at delivery. Of those randomized, 280 were HIV-negative and 52 were HIV-positive with CD4 count ≥200 cells/µL; 14 HIV-positive women with CD4 count <200 cells/µL were followed in an observational cohort because they were to receive daily cotrimoxazole per national guidelines.

interventions.extracted: Women were randomized to receive either daily cotrimoxazole prophylaxis (CTX; 2 tablets containing sulfamethoxazole 400 mg and trimethoprim 80 mg) or intermittent preventive treatment with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP; Fansidar) during pregnancy. The trial was open-label, with CTX taken daily at home after the first supervised dose and SP administered directly under observation at antenatal visits. HIV-positive women with CD4 counts <200 cells/µl were not randomized; they received routine daily CTX (and ART per national guidelines) as an observational cohort.

outcomes.extracted: Primary outcome: placental malaria, assessed at delivery using placental blood/placental biopsy by microscopy and later molecular analysis (PCR on filter paper) and histopathology. Secondary outcomes: peripheral malaria parasitemia, assessed during monthly antenatal follow-up and at delivery by peripheral blood smear; perinatal mortality, assessed from delivery through 6 weeks postpartum; birth weight, measured in kilograms at delivery and used to classify low birth weight (<2,500 g) at birth; gestational age at delivery, estimated from last menstrual period or developmental score at delivery and used to define preterm delivery (<37 completed weeks); spontaneous abortion (≤28 weeks gestation), ascertained through pregnancy follow-up; stillbirth (>28 weeks gestation), ascertained at delivery; neonatal mortality (≤28 days after birth), ascertained during postnatal follow-up; maternal mortality up to 6 weeks postpartum; major and minor birth defects, assessed at delivery and early postnatal follow-up; maternal anemia, measured by hemoglobin during pregnancy (once in early third trimester, 30–34 weeks) and at delivery; safety outcomes including adverse events and serious adverse events, recorded throughout monthly antenatal visits, at delivery, and at 1 and 6 weeks postpartum.
